1: toigaroun kai hēmeis, tosoutos echontes perikeimenon hēmin nephon martyrōn — "therefore, since we are surrounded by so great a cloud of witnesses" (nephon martyrōn: cloud of witnesses — nephos: cloud, mass, great multitude; martyrōn: witnesses — genitive of apposition: a cloud that consists of witnesses): the martyres are witnesses not in the sense of spectators watching the current community's race but witnesses whose lives and deaths were themselves testimonies to faith (the sense of martyreō throughout Hebrews 11: to be approved, to testify). They witness by having witnessed — their completed lives are the evidence. Apothemenoi panta ogkon (having laid aside every weight): the athletic image of stripping for the race.
2: aphorōntes eis ton tēs pisteōs archēgon kai teleiōtēn Iēsoun — "looking to Jesus, the founder and perfecter of our faith" (aphorōntes: looking away to, looking off toward — the apo-prefix: looking away from distractions and fixing the gaze on Jesus; archēgon: pioneer/founder — same term as Hebrews 2:10; teleiōtēn: perfecter/completer — one who brings to the goal): hos anti tēs prokeimenēs autō charas hypemeinen stauron, aischynēs kataphronēsas (who for the joy set before him endured the cross, despising shame): anti: instead of or in exchange for; "for the joy" or "instead of the joy" — the ambiguity is between joy that motivated endurance and joy that was set aside for endurance. Both readings are grammatically possible; the former ("motivated by") is more commonly adopted.
4: Oupō mechri haimatos antikatesatēsate pros tēn hamartian antagōnizomenoi — "in your struggle against sin you have not yet resisted to the point of shedding blood" (antikatesatēsate: you stood against/resisted — the athletic compound of anti [against] + kata [down] + histēmi [stand]; antagōnizomenoi: struggling against — athletic combat vocabulary): the community has suffered but not yet martyrdom. The "not yet" places their experience below the level of the exemplars of Hebrews 11 (the sawn-asunder, the tortured) and certainly below the level of Jesus's cross — calibrating the community's perception of their own suffering.
5: kai eklellēsthe tēs parakelseos hētis hymin hōs huiois dialegetai — "and have you forgotten the exhortation that addresses you as sons?" (eklellēsthe: have you forgotten — perfect passive of eklanthanomai: to forget completely; hōs huiois dialegetai: speaks to you as sons — the present tense of ongoing address): the Proverbs 3:11-12 citation is not merely past encouragement but present address — Scripture continues to speak to the community in its current situation. The forgetting is the spiritual problem; the citation is the remedy.
10: hoi men gar pros oligas hēmeras kata to dokoun autois epaideuon, ho de epi to sympheron eis to metalabein tēs hagiōtētos autou — "for they disciplined us for a short time as it seemed best to them, but he disciplines us for our good, that we may share his holiness" (pros to sympheron: for the profitable/beneficial — the sympheron is the beneficial purpose vs. the arbitrary "seemed good to them" of human discipline; metalabein tēs hagiōtētos autou: to partake of/receive a share of his holiness — the goal of divine discipline is holiness-participation): the divine discipline is not arbitrary suffering but purposive formation toward the community's participation in the divine character.
11: pasa de paideia pros men to paron ou dokei charas einai alla lypēs, hysteron de karpon eirēnikon tois di' autēs gegymnasmenos apodidōsin dikaiosynēs — "for the moment all discipline seems painful rather than pleasant, but later it yields the peaceful fruit of righteousness to those who have been trained by it" (karpon eirēnikon: peaceful fruit — eirēnikon as an adjective modifying the fruit: the fruit has the quality of peace rather than turmoil; gegymnasmenos: having been trained/exercised — from gymnazō: to exercise naked, to train vigorously): dikaiosynēs is either subjective genitive (righteous fruit = the fruit that is righteousness) or qualitative (fruit of a righteous kind).
18: Ou gar proselēlythate psēlaphōmenō kai kekaumēnō pyri kai gnophō kai zophō kai thyellē — "for you have not come to what may be touched, a blazing fire and darkness and gloom and a tempest" (psēlaphōmenō: that may be touched, handled — perfect passive participle of psēlaphaō: to touch, feel, grope; the touchability of Sinai marks its earthly-material character; kekaumēnō pyri: blazing fire — perfect passive: fire that had been set burning and remains burning): the entire Sinai list is characterized as sensory, earthly, terrifying — a deliberate phenomenological contrast with the heavenly assembly of vv22-24.
22: alla proselēlythate Siōn orei kai polei theou zōntos, Ierousalēm epouraniō, kai myriasi angelōn, panēgyrei — "but you have come to Mount Zion and to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem, and to innumerable angels in festal gathering" (myriasi angelōn: myriads of angels — ten-thousands, uncountable; panēgyrei: festal gathering/assembly — the great civic or religious assembly for celebration): the distinction between panēgyrei (festal gathering of angels) and ekklēsia prōtotokōn (the assembly of the firstborn) has been debated: are these one group or two? Most take them as two separate groups within the heavenly city. Prōtotokōn (firstborn, plural): those who share the firstborn's inheritance — the community's heavenly enrollment is in the book of the firstborn-status believers.
24: kai diathēkēs neas mesitē Iēsou kai haimati rhantismou kreitton lalounti para ton Abel — "and to Jesus, the mediator of a new covenant, and to the sprinkled blood that speaks a better word than the blood of Abel" (diathēkēs neas: new covenant — here neas [numerically new, recent] rather than the kainēs [qualitatively new] of Hebrews 8:8, 13 and Hebrews 9:15; the variation may be for stylistic variety or to emphasize the covenant's temporal recency): haimati rhantismou (blood of sprinkling) — the OT background of the Mosaic covenant's inaugurating blood-sprinkling (Exodus 24:8; Hebrews 9:19-22), now applied to Christ's blood. Kreitton lalounti para ton Abel: speaking better than Abel — Abel's blood cried for justice; Christ's blood speaks forgiveness.
28: dio basileian asaleuton paralambanontes echōmen charin, di' hēs latreuōmen euarestōs tō theō meta eulabeias kai deous — "therefore let us be grateful for receiving a kingdom that cannot be shaken, and thus let us offer to God acceptable worship, with reverence and awe" (asaleuton: unshakeable, unable to be shaken — the negation of the saleusō from Haggai 2:6 cited in v26): the unshakeable kingdom received through the Christological fulfillment of Haggai's shaking-promise is the basis of the community's gratitude-worship. Eulabeias kai deous: reverence and awe — the two terms of the appropriate human posture before the consuming-fire God (v29).
29: kai gar ho theos hēmōn pyr katanaliskōn — "for our God is a consuming fire" (pyr katanaliskōn: consuming fire — citing Deuteronomy 4:24 LXX exactly): the closing verse applies the divine consuming-fire character to the new covenant community's worship-life. The God who is consuming fire is not abandoned in the new covenant but approached with greater access — the approach requires the greater reverence that the Son's blood makes possible and necessary.
3: analogisasthe gar ton toiautēn hypomenēkota hypo tōn hamartoōlōn eis heauton antilogian — "consider him who endured such opposition from sinners against himself, so that you will not grow weary and lose heart" (analogisasthe: reckon up, consider carefully — aorist imperative of analogizomai: to reason through, to calculate analogically; the ana-prefix suggests careful re-counting): antilogian: contradiction, opposition, hostile speech — anti + logia: speaking-against; the word covers verbal hostility, contradiction, and the formal legal challenge. Kamēte tais psychais hymōn eklyomenoi (lest you become weary, fainting in your souls): eklyō: to release the tension, to go slack — the opposite of the taut focus on Jesus that the verse urges.
6: hon gar agapāi Kyrios paideuei, mastigoi de panta huion hon paradechetai — "because the Lord disciplines the one he loves, and he chastens everyone he accepts as his son" (Proverbs 3:12 LXX): mastigoi: scourges, flogs — a stronger word than paideuei (disciplines); the Proverbs text escalates from discipline to scourging to convey the full force of corrective love. Hon agapāi... hon paradechetai (the one he loves... the one he receives/accepts): the divine discipline is restricted to those within the relationship — it is a mark of belonging, not rejection. The parallel structure of the two verbs (discipline/love; scourge/accept) makes the same point twice with different vocabulary.
7: eis paideian hypomenete; hōs huiois hymin prospheretai ho theos; tis gar huios hon ou paideuei patēr — "endure hardship as discipline; God is treating you as his children. For what children are not disciplined by their father?" (eis paideian hypomenete: endure with a view to/for discipline — the eis gives the purpose: endurance is not mere stoicism but intentional orientation toward the formative goal): hōs huiois hymin prospheretai ho theos (God deals with you as with sons — prospheretai: present tense ongoing dealing): the suffering is not evidence of abandonment but evidence of the Father's engaged parental attention. The rhetorical question (tis gar huios: for what son?) assumes universal affirmative: all sons receive discipline.
8: ei de chōris este paideias, hēs metochoi gegonasi pantes, ara nothoi kai ouch huioi este — "if you are not disciplined — and everyone undergoes discipline — then you are not legitimate children but illegitimate" (nothoi: illegitimate, bastard-born — a legal term for a child born outside of recognized marriage; ouch huioi: not sons [in the legal sense of recognized, inheriting sons]): the social reality behind the word: a nothos child was not educated by the father and did not share in the father's estate. The absence of discipline signals the absence of the paternal relationship — not divine indifference but non-belonging.
9: eita tous men tēs sarkos hēmōn pateras eichomen paideutos kai enetrepometha; ou poly mallon hypotakēsometha tō patri tōn pneumatōn kai zēsomen — "moreover, we have all had human fathers who disciplined us and we respected them for it. How much more should we submit to the Father of spirits and live" (pateras tēs sarkos: fathers of the flesh — the bodily, genetic fathers in contrast to ton patera tōn pneumatōn [the Father of spirits]): enetrepometha: we respected, showed deference — entrepō: to turn toward with respect; the a minori ad maius argument: if earthly-biological fathers earned submission through imperfect discipline, the Father whose essence is Spirit and whose discipline is perfect deserves infinitely more. Kai zēsomen (and we will live): submission to the Father of spirits produces life — the submission is not mere social compliance but life-giving covenantal response.
12: dio tas paralelymenas cheiras kai ta paralyelymena gonata anorthōsate — "therefore, strengthen your feeble arms and weak knees" (Isaiah 35:3 echoed): paralelymenas cheiras: hands that have been made slack/drooping — paralyelymenas: perfect passive of paralyō: to release the side tension, to go limp; the cognate of our word "paralysis": paralelymenas gonata (paralyzed/drooping knees): the two body-parts that fail under sustained exertion — the hands that lose their grip and the knees that buckle. The command is paradoxical: the drooping hands are told to strengthen themselves. The imperatives assume that the community has agency even in exhaustion — the call to action is itself a form of strengthening.
13: kai trochas orthas poieite tois posin hymōn, hina mē to chōlon ektrapē, iathē de mallon — "make level paths for your feet, so that the lame may not be disabled, but rather healed" (Proverbs 4:26 echoed; cf. Isaiah 35:3): trochas orthas poieite: make straight/level tracks — trochias: wheel-tracks, the ruts made by wheels; paths made straight enough for the injured to walk without stumbling. To chōlon: the lame one, the limping member — the community contains members who are weak and close to failing; the stronger members' straight-walking creates the conditions for the weaker to recover rather than collapse. Ektrapē... iathē: be turned aside... be healed — the choice of paths determines the outcome for the vulnerable.
14: Eirēnēn diōkete meta pantōn kai ton hagiasmon, hou chōris oudeis opsetai ton Kyrion — "make every effort to live in peace with everyone and to be holy; without holiness no one will see the Lord" (diōkete: pursue, chase — the verb of active hunting/pursuit; peace is not a passive condition but an active quest): hagiasmon: sanctification, consecration — the process and state of being set apart; not merely moral improvement but the condition of being appropriately related to the holy God. Hou chōris oudeis opsetai ton Kyrion: without which no one will see the Lord — the eschatological vision of God (the beatific vision) is conditioned on holiness. The warning echoes Psalms 24:3-4 (who may stand in the holy place? Those with clean hands and pure hearts).
15: episkopountes mē tis husterōn apo tēs charitos tou theou, mē tis riza pikrias anō phyousa enochla kai di' autēs mianthōsin hoi polloi — "see to it that no one fails to obtain the grace of God and that no bitter root grows up to cause trouble and defile many" (episkopountes: overseeing, looking over carefully — the community exercise of pastoral mutual-care; husterōn apo tēs charitos: falling short of, falling behind from the grace of God — hysterein apo: to be separated from by falling behind): riza pikrias: root of bitterness (Deuteronomy 29:17 LXX): the agricultural metaphor of a poisonous root spreading underground and contaminating what grows from it; bitterness is insidious because it operates beneath the surface and spreads before it is visible. Mianthōsin hoi polloi: the many be defiled — the singular root affects the corporate community.
16: mē tis pornos ē bebēlos hōs Ēsau, hos anti brōseōs mias apedeto ta prōtotokia heautou — "see that no one is sexually immoral, or is godless like Esau, who for a single meal sold his inheritance rights as the oldest son" (bebēlos: profane, godless — one who crosses into the sacred realm with improper foot, who treats what is set-apart as common; brōseōs mias: a single eating/meal — brōsis: the eating-act, food): Esau is paired with pornos (sexually immoral) not because the two sins are equated but because both represent the exchange of the permanent for the immediate-sensory. Prōtotokia: birthright, firstborn privileges — the entire future inheritance-claim exchanged for a bowl of stew. The sacrilege (bebēlos) is the measure by which Hebrews frames Esau: not sexual but covenantal failure.
17: iste gar hoti kai metepeita thelōn klēronomēsai tēn eulogian apedokimasthē, metanoias gar topon ouch heuren, kaitoi meta dakryon ekzētēsas autēn — "for you know that afterward, when he wanted to inherit the blessing, he was rejected. Even though he sought the blessing with tears, he could not change what he had done" (apedokimasthē: was rejected — aorist passive of apodokimazō: to reject after testing, to pronounce unfit; cf. the rejected stone of Psalms 118:22): metanoias topon ouch heuren: found no place of repentance — the phrase is ambiguous: did he find no place to repent, or did he find no place to change the outcome? The Greek metanoia can mean either. In context, the topon (place) likely means the opportunity to reverse the outcome — the irreversibility of the birthright transaction is the pastoral warning.
19: kai salpiggos ēchō kai phōnē rhēmatōn, hēs hoi akousantes parētēsanto mē prostethēnai autois logon — "to the blast of a trumpet and to such a voice speaking words that those who heard it begged that no further word be spoken to them" (salpingos ēchō: the sound of a trumpet — Sinai's characteristic soundscape [Exodus 19:16, 19]): parētēsanto: begged off, asked to be excused — paraiteomai: to ask to be removed from something, to excuse oneself; the same verb as Hebrews 12:25 (do not refuse him who speaks). The people at Sinai could not bear the weight of direct divine address — their refusal is not disobedience but the recognition of creaturely incapacity before unmediated divine speech.
20: ouk epheron gar to diastelloumenon, Kan thērion thigē tou orous, lithobolēthēsetai — "because they could not bear what was commanded: If even an animal touches the mountain, it must be stoned" (Exodus 19:12-13): ouk epheron: they could not bear/carry — the weight of the divine command was unbearable; to diastelloumenon: what was commanded/ordered — the present passive participle of diasstellō: to command definitively, to give decisive orders. Kan thērion thigē: even if a beast touches — the most minimal, innocent contact with the mountain incurred capital punishment for the animal; the holiness-gradient at Sinai was absolute and death-dealing.
21: kai houtōs phoberotaton ēn to phantazomenon, Mōysēs eipen, Ekphobos eimi kai entromos — "the sight was so terrifying that Moses said, I am trembling with fear" (phoberotaton ēn to phantazomenon: the spectacle was absolutely terrifying — phoberōtatos is the superlative of phoberos: the most terrifying possible; phantazomenon: the appearing, the thing made visible — the theophanic spectacle): Ekphobos eimi kai entromos: I am struck with terror and trembling — the two compounds (ek-phobos: terror-struck; en-tromos: shaking-inwardly) together convey total physical and psychological overwhelm. The attribution to Moses of words from the Sinai theophany combines Deuteronomy 9:19 (Moses's fear at the golden-calf incident) with the Sinai context — a free composite citation.
23: ekklēsia prōtotokōn apographomenōn en ouranois, kai kritē theō pantōn, kai pneumasin dikaiōn teteleiōmenōn — "to the assembly of the firstborn, whose names are written in heaven. You have come to God, the Judge of all, to the spirits of the righteous made perfect" (prōtotokōn apographomenōn: of firstborn ones enrolled — apographō: to register, to enroll in a public census or citizenship list; the heavenly enrollment is the eschatological citizenship registration): ekklēsia prōtotokōn: the assembly of the firstborn — the plural prōtotokōn extends the firstborn status (held uniquely by the Son) to all who share in Christ's inheritance. Pneumasin dikaiōn teteleiōmenōn: spirits of the righteous who have been made perfect — the OT saints of Hebrews 11 now perfected in Christ (cf. Hebrews 11:40); they are pneumata (spirits) because their resurrection bodies are still awaited.
25: Blepete mē paraitēsēsthe ton lalounta; ei gar ekeinoi ouk exephygon epi gēs paraitēsamenoi ton chrēmatizonta, poly mallon hēmeis hoi ton ap' ouranōn apostraphentes — "see to it that you do not refuse him who speaks. If they did not escape when they refused him who warned them on earth, how much less will we, if we turn away from him who warns us from heaven" (paraitēsēsthe: refuse, beg off — aorist subjunctive; the same word used in v19 for Israel's refusal at Sinai): the structure is the familiar a minori ad maius argument: if the earthly theophany's rejection was fatal, the heavenly one's rejection is infinitely more so. Ton chrēmatizonta: the one who divinely warned — chrēmatizō: to receive a divine oracle, to be divinely instructed (cf. 8:5 of Moses). Ap' ouranōn apostraphentes: those turning away from heaven — the turn-away is directional, leaving the heavenly voice behind.
26: hou hē phōnē tēn gēn esaleusen tote, nyn de epēggeltai legōn, Eti hapax egō seiō ou monon tēn gēn alla kai ton ouranon — "at that time his voice shook the earth, but now he has promised: Once more I will shake not only the earth but also the heavens" (Haggai 2:6 LXX): esaleusen: shook — aorist; the Sinai earth-shaking is past and completed. Eti hapax: yet once more — the eschatological shaking is introduced by eti hapax: not a repeated event in the Sinai series but a final definitive shaking that goes beyond the earth to the heavens. Ou monon... alla kai (not only... but also): the scope of the coming shaking exceeds all previous ones — both created realms are shaken.
27: to de Eti hapax dēloi tēn tōn saleuomenōn metastasin hōs pepoiēmenōn, hina meinē ta mē saleuomena — "the words 'once more' indicate the removing of what can be shaken — that is, created things — so that what cannot be shaken may remain" (metastasin: removal, translation, transformation — from methistēmi: to remove, transfer, displace; a NT word for both physical removal and metaphorical transformation): tōn saleuomenōn vs. ta mē saleuomena: the shakeable vs. the unshakeable — the contrast structures the eschatological hermeneutic. Hōs pepoiēmenōn (as having been made — perfect passive participle): that which was made/created is by nature shakeable because it was made at a point in time from nothing. What was not made (the kingdom of v28) cannot be shaken because it does not belong to the created order.
